does: This paper applies sparse autoencoders to systematically extract interpretable features from the protein language model ESM-2. By training SAEs on ESM-2 embeddings across all layers, the work identifies thousands of human-interpretable latent features per layer that correlate strongly with established biological concepts including binding sites, structural motifs, and functional domains. The analysis reveals that while individual neurons in ESM-2 show limited alignment with known concepts (up to 46 per layer across 15 concepts), SAEs discover far richer structure: up to 2,548 interpretable features per layer. The paper additionally develops an automated pipeline using language models to interpret novel latent features that lack existing biological annotations, demonstrating that ESM-2 learns coherent concepts beyond current knowledge bases.
connects: This work extends the mechanistic interpretability paradigm from language models and neural networks into structural biology by adapting sparse autoencoders, a technique recently developed to decode superposition in transformer-based systems. It builds on growing recognition that protein language models capture rich biological information despite opaque internal representations, responding to fundamental questions about how PLMs encode structural and functional knowledge. The paper combines insights from interpretability research on feature extraction with classical protein bioinformatics, leveraging established protein annotation databases and structural biology concepts as ground truth for validation. It also connects to recent work demonstrating that SAEs can uncover interpretable features in large models more effectively than direct neuron analysis, suggesting PLMs represent concepts in superposed rather than disentangled form.
enables: This interpretability framework opens multiple directions for improving and extending protein language models. By mapping learned representations to biological concepts, the work enables better model design and targeted improvements for specific protein tasks like binding prediction and design. The automated interpretation pipeline for novel features could accelerate discovery of previously unknown protein properties and biological relationships encoded in PLMs. These interpretable features provide testable hypotheses for experimental validation and could guide rational protein engineering by revealing which latent concepts matter for function. The systematic cataloging of learned features also supports knowledge distillation, transfer learning, and building more reliable protein design systems where predictions are traceable to understandable mechanistic features rather than black-box embeddings.
